07 Data Fetching Patterns — Server Actions, React Query, and Streaming

← Back to lessons

Data fetching is where architecture decisions compound. The wrong pattern creates waterfalls, stale data, and loading state spaghetti. This lesson maps each approach to the problems it solves best.

The Decision Framework: Where Does the Data Live?

Before choosing a tool, ask two questions:

  1. When do you need the data? At build time (static), at request time (server), or after user interaction (client)?
  2. How often does it change? Never (truly static), occasionally (revalidate), or constantly (real-time)?
ScenarioPatternTool
Blog post contentStatic generationServer Component + fetch at build
Dashboard dataServer-side, per requestServer Component + no-cache fetch
Search results (as you type)Client-side, reactiveReact Query / SWR
Form submissionMutationServer Actions
Live comments/notificationsReal-timeWebSocket + client state
Infinite scroll feedPaginated clientReact Query + useInfiniteQuery

Pattern 1: Server Component Data Fetching

The simplest and most powerful pattern in the App Router. Fetch data directly in the component — no API routes, no useEffect, no loading state management.

// app/articles/page.tsx — Server Component (default)
async function ArticlesPage() {
  // Direct database query — runs on the server
  const articles = await db.article.findMany({
    where: { published: true },
    orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' },
    take: 20,
  });

  return (
    <div>
      <h1>Latest Articles</h1>
      {articles.map(article => (
        <ArticleCard key={article.id} article={article} />
      ))}
    </div>
  );
}

No useState. No useEffect. No loading state. No error handling boilerplate. The component is an async function that awaits data and returns JSX. The loading state comes from loading.tsx. Error handling comes from error.tsx. You just write the happy path.

The mental model: Server Components are like server-side templates (think PHP, Django views) but with React's component model and composition. You have the full power of the server (DB access, file system, environment variables) combined with React's component abstraction. It's the best of both worlds.

Avoiding waterfalls

// BAD: Sequential fetching (waterfall)
async function Dashboard() {
  const user = await getUser();        // 200ms
  const articles = await getArticles(); // 300ms — waits for user
  const stats = await getStats();       // 150ms — waits for articles
  // Total: 650ms

  return /* ... */;
}

// GOOD: Parallel fetching
async function Dashboard() {
  const [user, articles, stats] = await Promise.all([
    getUser(),        // 200ms
    getArticles(),    // 300ms — simultaneous
    getStats(),       // 150ms — simultaneous
  ]);
  // Total: 300ms (longest single fetch)

  return /* ... */;
}

Pattern 2: Streaming with Suspense

Suspense lets you show parts of the page immediately while slower data loads. Instead of waiting for everything, stream the UI as data arrives.

// app/articles/[slug]/page.tsx
import { Suspense } from 'react';

async function ArticlePage({ params }: Props) {
  // Fast: article content (usually cached)
  const article = await getArticle(params.slug);

  return (
    <article>
      <h1>{article.title}</h1>
      <p>{article.body}</p>

      {/* Slow: comments from a different service */}
      <Suspense fallback={<CommentsSkeleton />}>
        <CommentsSection articleId={article.id} />
      </Suspense>

      {/* Slow: related articles (ML recommendation) */}
      <Suspense fallback={<RelatedSkeleton />}>
        <RelatedArticles articleId={article.id} />
      </Suspense>
    </article>
  );
}

// Each async component fetches its own data
async function CommentsSection({ articleId }: { articleId: string }) {
  const comments = await getComments(articleId); // Slow API
  return <CommentsList comments={comments} />;
}

How streaming works under the hood: Next.js sends the initial HTML with skeleton/fallback UI immediately. When a Suspense boundary resolves, the server sends a small chunk of HTML and an inline script that swaps the skeleton with the real content. The browser progressively enhances the page. No client-side JavaScript needed for the swap — it's all HTML streaming.

Pattern 3: Server Actions for Mutations

Server Actions handle writes (creating, updating, deleting data). They're the modern replacement for API routes when the client needs to mutate server-side data.

// app/actions/articles.ts
'use server';

import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache';
import { redirect } from 'next/navigation';
import { z } from 'zod';

const ArticleSchema = z.object({
  title: z.string().min(1).max(200),
  body: z.string().min(10),
  category: z.enum(['politics', 'tech', 'culture']),
});

export async function createArticle(formData: FormData) {
  // 1. Validate input
  const parsed = ArticleSchema.safeParse({
    title: formData.get('title'),
    body: formData.get('body'),
    category: formData.get('category'),
  });

  if (!parsed.success) {
    return { errors: parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors };
  }

  // 2. Create in database
  const article = await db.article.create({
    data: parsed.data,
  });

  // 3. Invalidate caches
  revalidatePath('/articles');

  // 4. Redirect
  redirect(`/articles/${article.slug}`);
}
// Using Server Actions with useActionState (React 19+)
'use client';

import { useActionState } from 'react';
import { createArticle } from '@/app/actions/articles';

function CreateArticleForm() {
  const [state, formAction, isPending] = useActionState(createArticle, null);

  return (
    <form action={formAction}>
      <input name="title" required />
      {state?.errors?.title && (
        <p className="error">{state.errors.title}</p>
      )}

      <textarea name="body" required />
      {state?.errors?.body && (
        <p className="error">{state.errors.body}</p>
      )}

      <button type="submit" disabled={isPending}>
        {isPending ? 'Publishing...' : 'Publish'}
      </button>
    </form>
  );
}

Pattern 4: React Query for Client-Side Data

When data depends on user interaction — search queries, filters, pagination, infinite scrolling — you need client-side fetching. React Query (TanStack Query) handles the hard parts: caching, deduplication, background refetching, and stale data management.

// hooks/useArticles.ts
import { useQuery, useInfiniteQuery, useMutation } from '@tanstack/react-query';

// Basic query with automatic caching and refetching
function useArticles(category: string) {
  return useQuery({
    queryKey: ['articles', category],  // Cache key (like a database index)
    queryFn: () => fetch(`/api/articles?category=${category}`).then(r => r.json()),
    staleTime: 5 * 60 * 1000,         // Data is fresh for 5 minutes
  });
}

// Infinite scroll
function useArticleFeed() {
  return useInfiniteQuery({
    queryKey: ['feed'],
    queryFn: ({ pageParam = 0 }) =>
      fetch(`/api/feed?cursor=${pageParam}`).then(r => r.json()),
    getNextPageParam: (lastPage) => lastPage.nextCursor,
  });
}

// Mutation with optimistic update
function useLikeArticle() {
  const queryClient = useQueryClient();

  return useMutation({
    mutationFn: (articleId: string) =>
      fetch(`/api/articles/${articleId}/like`, { method: 'POST' }),
    onMutate: async (articleId) => {
      // Optimistically update the cache
      await queryClient.cancelQueries({ queryKey: ['articles'] });
      const previous = queryClient.getQueryData(['articles']);

      queryClient.setQueryData(['articles'], (old: Article[]) =>
        old.map(a => a.id === articleId
          ? { ...a, likes: a.likes + 1, liked: true }
          : a
        )
      );

      return { previous };
    },
    onError: (err, id, context) => {
      // Rollback on error
      queryClient.setQueryData(['articles'], context?.previous);
    },
    onSettled: () => {
      // Refetch to get server truth
      queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['articles'] });
    },
  });
}

React Query vs Server Components: They're not competitors — they're complements. Use Server Components for initial page data (fast, no JS shipped). Use React Query for data that changes based on user interaction (search, filters, infinite scroll, real-time updates). The initial data from the server can hydrate React Query's cache, giving you the best of both worlds.

Combining Server and Client Patterns

// Hydrate React Query from Server Component data
// app/articles/page.tsx (Server Component)
import { dehydrate, HydrationBoundary, QueryClient } from '@tanstack/react-query';

export default async function ArticlesPage() {
  const queryClient = new QueryClient();

  // Prefetch on the server
  await queryClient.prefetchQuery({
    queryKey: ['articles', 'all'],
    queryFn: () => db.article.findMany({ take: 20 }),
  });

  return (
    <HydrationBoundary state={dehydrate(queryClient)}>
      <ArticleList />  {/* Client component — uses useQuery */}
    </HydrationBoundary>
  );
}

// components/ArticleList.tsx (Client Component)
'use client';

function ArticleList() {
  // This reads from the hydrated cache — no loading state!
  const { data: articles } = useQuery({
    queryKey: ['articles', 'all'],
    queryFn: fetchArticles,
  });

  // Client-side interactions work seamlessly
  const [filter, setFilter] = useState('all');
  // ...
}

Check Your Understanding

1. When should you use a Server Component for data fetching vs React Query?

Server Components for initial page load data that doesn't depend on user interaction (article content, user profile, dashboard stats). React Query for data that changes based on client-side interactions (search, filters, pagination, infinite scroll, real-time updates).

2. You have three data fetches in one Server Component. What's the fastest way to fetch them?

Promise.all([fetch1(), fetch2(), fetch3()]). This runs all three fetches in parallel instead of sequentially. The total time is the duration of the slowest fetch, not the sum of all three.

3. What does Suspense do in the context of streaming SSR?

It lets the server send the page HTML in chunks. Content outside Suspense boundaries ships immediately. When a Suspense boundary resolves (async data arrives), the server streams a chunk with the resolved HTML and a script that swaps the fallback. The user sees a progressively loading page.

4. What's an optimistic update and when would you use one?

An optimistic update immediately applies the expected result of a mutation to the UI before the server confirms it. You use it for actions where the success rate is very high and instant feedback matters (like/unlike, adding to cart, toggling a setting). If the server returns an error, you roll back to the previous state.

Exercise: Build a Search with Multiple Patterns

  1. Create an articles page with initial data from a Server Component
  2. Add a client-side search that uses React Query to fetch filtered results
  3. Hydrate the React Query cache from the server data (zero loading state on first load)
  4. Add optimistic "bookmark" functionality using useMutation
  5. Wrap slow-loading sections in Suspense with skeleton fallbacks
